Which is why yoga chikitsa is not just a quick fix for every problem that we have. Again, yoga chikitsa yoga therapy doesn't believe in one pill for one ill or one sizefits all because yoga chikitsa approaches the human being. This is the celtogenic orientation of antonowski which is the swastia approach of Indian healing traditions where the person is at the center of our therapeutic process and not the disease. So I think the fundamental secret of yoga therapy which is the title of today is that it is not disease oriented but it is person centered. The second secret it is not outcome oriented it is process oriented. So yoga as a therapy and yoga chikita is the appropriate term is process oriented personentric and is celtogenic in orientation. It shares a lot of commonalities with some beautiful concepts that I have got to love over many decades. The first concept is one that was postulated and brought out well by my dear Albert Einstein who said science and religion I paraphrase it science and spirituality are two eyes and science without religion as he put it is lame. him religion without science is blind and you need both. So I think one of the first and foremost concepts that I would like to share today is it is not science or spirituality but it is science and spirituality. So this is a very important point to keep in mind. Science and spirituality. There's no dichotomy. There's no division. When I look through one eye, I have one perspective. When I look through the other eye, I have another perspective. When I look through both, I create a new dimension. That holistic dimension is the dimension adopted by yoga as a therapy. Yoga chikita. It is not reductionist. It is inclusive. Modern science, modern medicine often reduces the person to organs, systems and today we go all the way down into the molecular mechanics and dimensions of health and disease. So it is very microscopic. It is constantly looking at the small picture and magnifying the small picture so you can understand the small picture better more and look at it in greater detail. The yoga dishi is taking the opposite perspective the contrary perspective. It says you are not just your cells. You are not just your organs or your organ systems. You are not just this physical body but you are a body that functions. You have a body that is functional and capacity for psychological dimensions. More than that as a human being you are endowed with the power of choice and then no man well no woman no person is an island. We are all interconnected. Which is why one earth one health understands that it is not just human health that we need to focus on but health of the entire planet and every being that exists on this blue planet. Yoga chikita tries to understand what is at the root of our duka, our suffering. That root cause analysis I think is best provided by the yogic philosophy, the yogic psychology and the yogic spirituality. Swami Ganandhagi my beloved father and guru he would say the first disease is the disease of duality where you feel different than the universe in which you exist. The moment you feel separate from the universe in which you exist, duka has started. Because separation creates duality, duality creates friction. Friction creates heat. Heat creates its own suffering. And the whole downward spiral of the humankind begins with separation. When we talk of yoga, our Vedas, our opishads give us so much of food for thought. The brahavanyaka, the largest, most expansive says from duality comes fear. From separation comes fear. And once fear comes, all your survival mechanisms, the stress mechanisms come into play. And once they come into play, congratulations. Duka will manifest sooner than later in greater and greater magnitude. The yogic philosophy, the yogic psychology and spirituality says, we are human beings. But at the same time, we have forgotten our divinity. We are individuals who have forgotten our universality. And this is why to paraphrase Mahavishi Patanjali, we are the divine suffering temporary amnesia. We are the divine suffering temporary amnesia and we have forgotten we are the divine. We are the universe. We think we are the drop. We have forgotten we are the ocean. Why? Because of the clatas, the clias which Mahavishi Patanjali postulates are at the root of all human suffering and any root cause analysis in yoga chikitsa must and I'm going to underline that with green, blue, pink, whatever highlights you like. Every root cause analysis in yoga chikitsa must include the clasia model of maharavish patanjali. It is the best model to understand human suffering duka. Why do we have duka? Um I think that the question that you just asked me is um just very much related to what we were just talking about how a lot of the time within psychology and also in yoga there is a lot of emphasis on empirical validation. And so I as a clinical psychologist am often asked you know to use empirically valid treatments in my work with people. So u I thought I would share just a few. So Dr. Swami Ganandiri talks about how yoga therapy is the return of the mind. Uh so the return of mind that feels separated from the universe in which it exists. And I really love that quote because it comes back to what Dr. Anandi was saying at the beginning um about the importance of recognizing that human beings all of us are actually already whole. And when we perceive people in this way, it invites a certain level of compassion for ourselves and for the person that we're sitting with as well. And um it's a less harshly pathizing way of working with people and it's also person- centered as Dr. Anand G was saying uh as well as humanistic. So people are are seen automatically as inherently good at their core and as having a natural urge and tendency to want to grow and self-actualize. Um, so I really love that definition because in my work with people in trying to blend the yoga therapy in an empirically valid way with other psychology interventions, starting off with that framework is very helpful. Seeing people as whole and looking to see what are the processes um or different ways of being that are contributing to them feeling out of alignment. And so a couple of other quotes that also overlap with u my understanding of yoga therapy and why I think it's important to bring in yoga therapy with clinical work in psychology. Um one is by Dr. Anand G that he shares quite often which is if it doesn't empower the individual from within it is not yoga therapy. So we've been talking a lot about how we are all interconnected with everything else. And what I love about the gitananda tradition and the way yoga chikitsa is taught here is that it's an extremely empowering way of working with people. The emphasis is on helping people to feel that they have the power to bring changes into their lives on their own. And that doesn't mean that they don't ask for help or connect with other people, but the emphasis is on looking within yourself first and seeking to understand who you are and who you are as a unique soul and then from from going inward to outward to the way that you then interact with the world. So it's just uh I love that emphasis of looking within first and then that's why the one of the practice mottos at inner thriving holistic psychology is flourish from the inside out because I feel as though that emphasis on looking within first is so important um and enlightening and the last uh quote that I thought I would share that it's Dr. Dr. Nanj's interpretation of a Bhagavag Gita passage and it's lift the self by the self and so the first self is with a small S and the other S is with a bigger S. So it's indicating that we have that ability to look within ourselves in order to grow and evolve. It's all coming from within and going outward. And yes, even though we will want to connect with other people and uh recognize that connection more deeply, it often does start with going in and lifting the self purposefully with intention from the inside out. Um, so those were just some quotes that inform why I think it's important to include yoga therapy into psychology work. And to answer the question that you asked more specifically about how I can integrate evidence-based psychological approaches with yogic tools from the Gitananda tradition. Um there are a few ways some of which are a little bit more um implicit. So I think it starts at the level of when I'm first meeting with clients coming back to that humanistic person- centered way of interacting with people. So um the gitananda yoga chikitsa tradition really emphasizes listening for specific information and observing specific things. So going back to that the diagnostics that we use we are observing the way people are moving their tone of voice uh their mannerisms the words that they're not saying the words that they are saying. And yes, in clinical psychology, we're also trained to do that. This is a little bit part of our work in the clinical interviewing process. But within the Gitananda Yoga Chikitsa school, I feel like it's even more deeply ingrained uh that we're looking for very specific things. So, or different behaviors when we're observing people. And so for example we might be looking for as I said patterns of speech movements how people are moving etc. And that can really help us to understand the person on a bigger level and then also it can help us to later use interventions that are tailored to that individual more specifically. So it helps us to develop hypotheses about the kinds of interventions that would be good to use. So things like looking for the energy that the people are exuding tamasic, rajasic, satvic and then having understood that later being able to help with specific interventions that can balance those energies. Um, another way that again is a little bit more implicit or subtle in terms of how I can bring yoga therapy into my work with people is through understanding exactly what Dr. Anaji was saying at the beginning that u individuals are very very complex. There's a tendency sometimes to try to box people in especially when we give diagnosis. we can make assumptions about the kinds of interventions that might help that particular person. Um, but in the Gitanji yoga tradition, it's very much ingrained in us to understand that there really isn't a one-sizefits-all approach to working with people. And so in that initial intake, in those initial intakes, when we're doing this, getting to know the person and observing certain things and listening for certain certain um ways of describing that the client may have, we're also staying attuned to what the root cause of the misalignment that the person is describing might be. Um, and so, um, if we have a person, for example, that I've diagnosed with depression and we notice that they have all of the characteristic symptoms of that disorder, um, we would not necessarily make the assumption that we would just use one particular mode of therapy for them. Again, not a one-sizefits-all model. There are many individuals who are experiencing depression for various different reasons. It could be because of an overdoing in the world, over approach uh versus a withdrawing. And understanding what that root cause of the disorder is is just really important in terms of being able to tailor the types of yoga therapy as well as psychological interventions that we use. ultimately. Um, and then the third way that I thought of to share uh is a little bit more specific. So, uh, in terms of how I would integrate yoga therapy into psychotherapy work. So, there are some specific interventions that are again empirically validated by a lot of scientific research. Um and so one way that we can bring the yoga therapy in is through using it as an adjunct to what is already being addressed in the psychotherapy. So uh cognitive behavioral therapy is one of the most researched many people probably know of it. It's one of the most researched psychotherapies. Um, and the model is depicted as a triangle where it's believed that thoughts are impacting emotions and behaviors and those arrows that are connecting and influencing each other are birectional. But what's missing in that model is the physiological component. And so one way as I was saying that we can incorporate more of a yogic lens yoga therapy is through recognizing what is not addressed in one type of therapy in clinical psychology and then bringing it in. So addressing the psychological the sorry physiological component uh which is not being addressed so much within CBT. Um, we know and what I understand from the Gitananda tradition and from practicing yoga myself is that one of the best or one of the most beautiful contributions of yoga therapy is the ability to teach people skills and tools for relaxing and grounding. And so there are just so many tools that we can use to help people do that and we can add that into an intervention that's already existing. Um, another way that we can bring in the yoga therapy in a way that would be seen as more empirically valid is um through looking at the pract the interventions the psychology interventions that are already getting a lot of backing and just noticing that a lot of the processes that are being targeted are already actually yoga essentially. So one of the the one of the types of therapy that are used a lot more that is used a lot more nowadays is called acceptance and commitment based therapy and it is a thirdwave type of psychotherapy that is an offshoot of cognitive behavioral therapy and um this particular therapy emphasizes process-based work like Dr. Dr. Nandi was also talking about earlier. So the emphasis is more on the processes that are going on contributing to suffering and there are six processes within ACT that are emphasized as important to address and to work with in order to regain a sense of alignment or what is called psychological flexibility. Psychological flexibility is seen as um a mark of health and within the ACT acceptance and commitment based therapy approach any psychological inflexibility is seen as contributing to um disease or lack of wellness. Um and so um there are six processes involved in ACT and four of the six are mindfulness. So one of them is acceptance. Another one is you know creating distance between the thoughts that are coming up uh not hooking on to them in the mind creating some distance non-judgmentally in the mind between the types of thoughts that are showing up etc. And so another way that we can bring in the yoga in a way that would be seen as more empirically valid is by using skills that are already inherent to Gitanji yoga tradition that facilitate mindfulness. And there are many and I don't know that uh you know mindfulness is essentially yoga. It's just a term that was created by Dr. John Kabitzin primarily. Um but um it really stems from Eastern philosophy and from yoga. So there are many practices that we can work with within the Gita Ganandanda tradition that would be acting to facilitate mindfulness, acceptance and ability to sit with difficult emotions, make room for them, all of which are targets within ACT already, if that makes sense. So those were some of the ideas that I had um around how to incorporate yoga therapy in a way that would be seen as empirically valid. So hopefully that made sense and uh I'll stop there for now.
